data.title: 5 Signs Why Investors Should Be More Cautious
data.description:
Investors had quickly forgotten the three percent plunge in the NASDAQ (COMP:IND) last week, which was the worse weekly performance for the tech heavy index since early April. This is when the administration announced "reciprocal tariffs." The NASDAQ clawed back three quarters of last week's losses on Monday after a resolution to the longest federal government shutdown in U.S. history. Equities did have a big selloff on Thursday, largely on growing worries that the Fed might not cut rates again during the December FOMC meeting.

I believe window dressing and other seasonal factors such as the traditional "Santa Claus Rally" could easily keep equities at or near all-time highs through yearend. However, all bets are off as 2026 commences. That is when investor complacency could come back to haunt them. Equities remain heavily in overbought territory viewed via a variety of traditional valuation metrics. In addition, the jobs market is clearly starting to falter in recent months, and most consumers are struggling.

In today's column, I am highlighting five things investors should be much more concerned about when assessing the overall markets as well as the U.S. economy.

#1 - No Equity Risk Premium

One of the basic tenets of Investing 101 is that investors put money in the market to garner better longer-term returns than from other potential investments like bonds or treasury bills. In return, they accept higher short-term risks. In short, equities provide a risk premium over safer investments. However, not only is there no "equity-risk premium" at current trading levels, this "premium"' is actually negative.

#2 - Minuscule Dividend Yields

The equity risk premium above is calculated by taking the inverse of the current P/E ratio of the S&P 500 (SP500) minus the current "risk free" 10-Year treasury yield (US10Y). As can be seen in the chart above, the market is in uncharted territory. The current extreme valuation environment equities currently inhabit can also be found in the minuscule dividend yield on the S&P 500.

Again, we are in uncharted territory as the S&P 500 dividend yield has only been this low once below. At the very tail end of the Internet Boom. Now one can argue that stock buybacks are a more tax efficient way to return cash to shareholders. It also helps corporate managers hit their EPS goals that trigger annual stock grants and options. However, even stock buybacks could ebb in the quarters ahead as many large firms divert more funds to capital expenditures for the massive AI infrastructure buildout across the nation.

#3 - Job Growth Going Negative

Second, the jobs markets are going from bad to worse. Job growth slowed dramatically over the summer. With revisions, there was monthly job growth of just under 30,000 positions from June to August.

Now it looks job growth has gone negative. The government shutdown prevented the September and October BLS jobs reports from being published, and it doesn't look they will be generated at all even as the federal workforce returns to their offices. Economists surveyed by Dow Jones did estimate some 60,000 jobs were lost in October, it should be noted.

In addition, ADP just reported that U.S. firms were shedding more than 11,000 jobs a week through late October. Goldman Sachs also just came out with a projection that 50,000 jobs were lost last month. It is clear that the U.S. labor market has gone from an environment where there was little hiring but little firing going on. Now, it appears there is little hiring but some additional layoff activity. What happens in 2026 when AI capabilities are increasingly integrated into corporations to make their operational processes more efficient?

#4 - Consumer Loan Delinquencies Soaring

Given the deteriorating jobs markets and much higher interest rates since the Federal Reserve starting to reverse its ZIRP policies in early 2022, it is hardly surprising that consumer loan delinquencies are soaring. Non-prime auto loan delinquencies are hitting record highs. Subprime auto loans that were past due at least 60 days hit 6.65% in October according to Fitch. This is the highest rate posted since they started keeping records in the early 1990s. Higher than the peak in the Great Financial Crisis. Auto repossessions are tracking to at least three million in 2025. The most since 2009.

Student loan delinquencies have also spiked since repayment and collections were restarted this year after a four-year taxpayer funded hiatus. There is nearly $1.8 trillion worth of U.S. student loan debt outstanding, owed by just over 40 million Americans. According to a recent report in October, there are roughly 5.3 million borrowers that are in default (over 270 days delinquent). In addition, there are another 4.3 million borrowers that are between 181 and 270 days late on their payments.

Credit card debt has recently hit a record $1.21 trillion. And as you can see from the chart above, delinquency rates on credit card loans are moving significantly higher across the board, but especially for low-income consumers. Rising loan delinquencies and a worsening jobs market is a toxic combination for consumer sentiment. And consumer activity contributes nearly 70% of U.S. GDP.

#5 - A Picture Is Worth A Thousand Words

I leave investors with the encompassing market analysis that incorporates various valuation metrics to paint a picture on how overvalued the market has become, viewed from a historical lens.

Despite my increasingly negative view on the overall market, I have learned one thing over four decades of investing. This is that the market can stay irrational longer than you can stay solvent. Michael Burry seems to be the latest fund manager to "rediscover" that maxim. This is why only 1% of my portfolio is in what I have dubbed Armageddon trades or long dated, deep out of the money bear put spreads that will produce massive returns. However, only if the overall market falls by 20% to 30% over the next 12-15 months. I view this as cheap portfolio insurance. 25% of my holdings are in cash/short-term treasuries and the rest in covered call positions around the few stocks I am finding with reasonable valuations.

data.title: Nebius Q3: There's No AI Bubble
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Nebius (NBIS) crushed earnings in my opinion, despite a mild revenue miss. Management has guided towards $7-$9 billion ARR, which is more than anyone could have expected.

And yet, the stock has sold off and now trades below $100.

This won’t last.

Yes, there’s dilution risk, but even accounting for this, the stock is undervalued. Nebius has become the undeniable king of hyperscalers, and at today’s price, the company is a steal.

Q3 Was Undeniable

It’s difficult to believe results like these, and yet Nebius just posted this monster quarter.

Revenues up 355% YoY and 39% QoQ, with a slight revenue miss, due to the fact that all of their capacity has sold out.

The only limiting factor seems to be how fast Nebius can scale. Nebius expects to reach 2.5 GW of contracted power by 2026, guiding for $7-$9 billion ARR by the end of next year. This represents a 15x increase from today’s levels.

But while this may seem overly optimistic, consider the fact that management claims most of this capacity has already been sold.

We already know about the OpenAI deal, and during the earnings call, the company unveiled a further $3 billion deal with Meta (META).

There’s no denying that the demand is there, and Nebius is delivering.

Why The Sell-Off?

Of course, this must be the biggest question on investors’ minds right now.

First, great earnings were already anticipated, and Nebius has increased more than 5x in the last few months.

Second, a big deal announcement was expected already, and perhaps the $3 billion deal with Meta was less than the market wanted.

Third, Nebius did increase its capex guidance, and it will be funding this through an ATM share offering, which could dilute shareholders to the tune of 10%.

Last, there’s been a general pullback in the hyperscaler sector, with investors worrying about competition and long-term profitability.

What Sets Nebius Apart?

Yes, I agree that the market is crowded and valuations are rich. But as I have mentioned before, Nebius stands head and shoulders above the rest thanks to its integrated AI stack.

On the latest earnings call, we can get a further glimpse of why Nebius is, at least in my opinion, the best hyperscaler.

We released our new enterprise-ready cloud platform version 3.0 called Aether and our new inference platform called Nebius Token Factory.

Source: Arkday, Nebius CEO

Aether is Nebius’s enterprise-grade AI cloud platform designed to help large organizations run mission-critical AI workloads with the security, control, and reliability they expect from a top-tier cloud provider.

It introduces full compliance, governance, and observability features, including identity and access management, advanced monitoring, and enterprise security certifications that most neoclouds lack.

On the other hand, Token Factory brings inference, post-training, and access management into one unified system, allowing developers to take models from experiment to production without stitching together multiple tools.

It supports leading open models, including Nvidia’s Nemotron, OpenAI’s GPT-OSS, Meta’s Llama, DeepSeek, and Alibaba’s Qwen, while promising to cut inference costs and latency by up to 70%.

As we can see, Nebius is more than hardware and, in many ways, is at the cutting edge of this technology.

How Much Is Nebius Really Worth?

I’ve valued Nebius before, and here’s my updated valuation with what we know now.

I have accounted for the dilution and the new revenue guidance.

On the lower end of guidance, we have the bear case, with Nebius achieving $7 billion in ARR and $4.5 billion in actual revenue for 2027.

At an EBITDA margin of 16%, below estimates, $1.5 billion in equity value for its other businesses, and an EV/EBITDA of 22, we get a target price of $63.

On the more bullish side, with $9 billion ARR, a more generous margin, and multiple, we have a bullish stock price of $151.

At today’s price of around $90, I think the upside outweighs the downside.

Technical Analysis

The stock’s attempting to put in a double bottom at around $90, where we have some volume support.

If we break this, then $70 is in play, which would be the 61.8% retracement of the rally and just above the 200-week EMA.

At that price, I would happily double down on my NBIS position.

Risks

As discussed above, competition, execution, and even other limiting factors like energy demand could be a problem going forward.

But Nebius isn’t making up its numbers. The demand is there, at least for now, and we should value the company as such.

Final Thoughts

Nebius is still the best hyperscaler out there, in my opinion, and it has become much cheaper after earnings. Obviously, because the price is down, but also because fundamentals have improved substantially.

Don’t sweat this sell-off, and be ready to back up the truck at $70; that’s my recommendation.

data.title: Lululemon: Stock Price Is Down, But Moat Remains Intact
data.description:
Investment Thesis

After reporting Q2 2025 earnings and revising its guidance down from 5% to 7% to 2% to 4%, Lululemon Athletica's (LULU) stock price plunged by almost 18%. For the quarter, Lululemon reported revenue of $2.5 billion and an EPS of $3.1. While Lululemon convincingly beat consensus in EPS, it slightly missed the revenue expectation by 0.5%, which may have been one of the reasons for the market's negative reaction. Since then, Lululemon's stock price has recovered slightly from $150 per share to around $170 per share today. Despite this small recovery, Lululemon is still one of the worst stock performers in 2025.

However, being one of the worst-performing stocks is not always a bad thing as it opens up an opportunity to buy this still wonderful business at a wonderful price. Despite Lululemon's current ordeal, I believe that the company's moat remains intact. Its problems are fixable and transitory, and I expect that once the management's dedication to refocus on innovation and the market adjusts, the company will perform just as well. Furthermore, Lululemon still has an international growth story, which can offset the current slowdown of the US market.

The market's pessimistic reaction to the stock paved the way to a very attractive valuation. Therefore, I will call a 'Strong Buy' rating for this stock.

A Moat That Remains Intact

To assess whether the company has a moat, an observation of its historical return on equity capital is a good first step. A first-level assessment can generally be made by checking a company's historical return on equity.

Let's examine Lululemon's historical return on equity capital to assess whether it possesses a durable competitive advantage.

Since 2010, Lululemon's ROE has fluctuated between 20% and 30%-plus. While there's clear wide fluctuation in the company's ROE, there's no doubt that Lululemon is doing something phenomenal that allows it not only to generate a high return on equity capital but also to maintain it. Consistent high return equity capital for multiple years often indicates a moat as economic theory suggests that high returns on capital attract competition that reverts this excess return to the minimum cost of capital sufficient to still attract investors. Therefore, businesses that maintain their return on capital for a long period of time possess durable characteristics that fend off competition to protect their profit pool.

The next question I ask myself is a more qualitative one: 'What does it do that allows it to generate superb return on capital, and how likely is it to continue?'

Lululemon's Competitive Advantage

Now, what does Lululemon really do that allows it to generate a superb return on equity? To answer this question, we need to examine its strategy and how the company differentiates itself from its competitors. Lululemon is known for targeting female consumers, which traditional performance apparel brands like Nike(NKE) and Adidas(OTC:ADDYY) have historically underserved.

Lululemon strategically priced its product at a significant premium, usually around $100-plus. This enables the brand to take advantage of the consumer's tendency to substitute higher-priced products as an indication of high quality. This is a sort of shortcut thinking that consumers tend to do when buying a product.

Of course, just pricing a product at a high price isn't enough to exploit this tendency. A company still needs to match this price with product superiority to justify a premium price. This is where innovation comes into play, which Lululemon is known for. This is evident in Lululemon's management's tone during any earnings calls, where a considerable portion is allotted toward the innovations the company is introducing. While the company has admitted that it has relied too much on its stable franchise, which resulted in stale offerings, innovation has been the company's lifeblood for decades, and one that the company can easily reintroduce.

This ability to charge premium prices and compete in the higher-price market segment enables Lululemon to deliver consistent and superb margins.

High margins are not only an element that indicates a moat but also provide a cushion against market downturns. This cushion enables the company to continuously reinvest in the business, which positions the company well when the economy picks up, while continuously returning capital to shareholders. This enables investors to hold the stock with conviction during downturns. The buybacks initiated during favorable valuation will be value accretive to remaining shareholders in the coming years if the company continues to perform well.

A Distribution and Marketing Strategy Played to Its Finest

Another contributing factor that enables Lululemon to deliver superb returns on capital is its marketing and distribution strategy. The company's marketing strategy mainly revolves around collaborating with ambassadors who focus on certain niches, such as tennis, yoga, and running. The rise of social media influencers amplifies the effect of this strategy.

Now, why is this type of marketing strategy so potent and effective? Because it leverages the influencer or ambassador's authority.

Consumers are receptive to the influence of people with whom they share similarities. This tendency is amplified when the person they like is in a position of authority. A good example of this is when a person of authority, such as a yoga instructor, trainer, or coach, is using apparel from Lululemon or encouraging the use of Lululemon's apparel. Consumers use this as a shortcut to conclude that Lululemon's apparel is great for yoga. This works so effectively in sports such as basketball, where brands like Nike spend millions of dollars to get the athlete to use their equipment and apparel. The only difference with Lululemon's strategy is that the focus on smaller influencers and ambassadors makes their marketing spend more efficient. This is evident in the chart below.

While the chart's result can be skewed by the different accounting practices each company adopts, from an anecdotal view, athlete and celebrity endorsements are obviously more expensive than the smaller brand ambassadors with which Lululemon collaborates. This is not to say that Lululemon doesn't leverage celebrity and athlete endorsements, as the Lewis Hamilton collaboration certainly promises an expensive fee. But still, the company's core marketing strategy heavily relies on niche influencers, such as those in Yoga.

The hype this marketing strategy provides can also lead to social proof. If a consumer sees everyone wearing Lululemon apparel, they're most likely to follow. Social proof is so effective because it destroys the uncertainty or unknowns associated with any products, especially newer ones.

Another factor contributing to Lululemon's outperformance is its distribution strategy. Unlike other brands, Lululemon's method of reaching customers mainly involves its DTC channels, such as its company-owned physical stores and e-commerce.

One of the advantages of the wholesale channel is its access to a wide range of consumers, which brands can leverage to increase sales. However, a significant downside of wholesale is that the brand loses control over the shopping and customer experience and pricing, which is very important for brand building. While brands can exert influence on the wholesaler about their strategy in the shopping experience and pricing, the wholesaler will have the ultimate say. After all, it's the wholesaler that carries the inventory risk and not the brand. Despite a brand's insistence, they will often discount excessively to offload unsellable inventory to generate cash. Excessive discounting damages a brand's equity or value.

With Lululemon's premium positioning, it makes sense to rely less on wholesale and more on its DTC channel to have more control of its pricing, brand, and consumer experience. This control in distribution is another contributing factor that differentiates Lululemon from the competition.

How Likely Is This To Continue?

Now, how likely is this to continue? Based on my assessments of the company's earnings transcripts, I believe that there won't be a significant change in the company's strategy. The company's existing formula in innovation, marketing, and distribution will likely continue.

The small tweak I see is Lululemon's rededication to innovation to reintroduce newness in its product portfolio that's comparable to its historical standards, which I believe is a necessity due to the fast-changing nature of fashion. This won't be a hard shift for the company, as its innovativeness is inherent to the company's culture, which is what led to its market-leading position in the first place.

Competitive advantage is like a snowball; once it gets going, it's very hard to stop. This is why entrants or competitors rarely attack a strong incumbent without a differentiated capability or product. However, as the snowball gets bigger, the company often needs to reinvigorate itself. From time to time, the company's progress may peter out due to reasons mostly related to human nature, such as complacency, which years of success can instill into any company. The good news is this is a fixable problem as long as it didn't damage the company's brand equity.

The brand equity that it has built among women for decades remains strong. Brand equity is like a barrel of spirits. The more it ages, the more valuable it becomes.

Lululemon's revenue decline in its US segment can be concerning. In Q2 2025 earnings, Lululemon CEO Calvin McDonald said that the company now expects its US market to decline by 1% to 2% with the international segment growing at a double-digit rate. This may have been the effect of the company's undoing of relying too much on its stable franchise and less on innovation. Additionally, the company is also dealing with the effect of tariffs on its customers.

Despite the challenges in its US segment, Lululemon's international segment may offset this decline. Lululemon's market penetration in its international segment is still at low levels compared to the US. Therefore, there's a good chance that it could continue to deliver double-digit growth for the company, and this rapid growth rate could offset the stagnant US market.

The real question is whether Lululemon's playbook in the US market will work internationally. I believe the conditions that enable Lululemon's outstanding performance in the US are applicable to those of markets like Europe and Asia. The Consumer psychology that Lululemon has effectively leveraged also applies in these markets. The company may just need to slightly tweak its approach to adapt to the markets' unique culture, preferences, and tastes.

Valuation

Since the stock price plunged after the company's Q2 2025 earnings, Lululemon's stock price has undergone a slow recovery, rising from lows of $150 per share to around $170. Despite this price recovery, I still believe that Lululemon sells at an attractive valuation at a P/E multiple of around 12x.

This valuation is very attractive considering that similar companies sell at a significant premium. Of course, the P/E ratio is useless without the context of historical growth, as low-growth companies will naturally sell at a lower valuation than higher-growth ones.

Considering that Lululemon has significantly outperformed these companies in the past in terms of sales growth, I believe that it's excessive for such a company to sell at a significant discount relative to its similar peers. These are past performances, of course, and what matters is the market's expectation and judgment on the company's future growth prospects. But ask yourself this question: Does it make sense that Lululemon gets such audacious treatment when it still has significant international growth headroom compared to companies like Nike, which have all but penetrated all parts of the world with their performance shoes? I'm not a fan of extrapolating past performance into the future, but does it make sense that Lululemon, which has grown its top-line at a double-digit growth rate for almost a decade, gets such a low valuation?

While past revenue growth rate cannot be the sole vindication of future performance, it could still be a rough guide as it's indicative of the company's capability or skills to generate sales growth, which could translate into the future. While there can be a significant cause of concern after Lululemon's management guided for just a 4% to 6% growth in 2025, a far cry from its decade-long double-digit revenue growth, I believe that it's due to the significant external market shock from tariffs that's outside of the company's control. It's a matter of assessing whether this external shock has deteriorated or will deteriorate the successful economic franchise of Lululemon, which I believe is not the case. I believe that the current market conditions are transitory. Eventually, the market will adjust. As long as this transitory period doesn't cause Lululemon's brand equity to deteriorate, the company will likely prosper afterward.

Lastly, I believe the Lululemon brand is as valuable and comparable to well-known apparel brands such as Nike and Adidas. As mentioned earlier, a brand is like a spirit drink; it gets better and more precious as it ages. In the same way that Nike has become the representation of basketball shoes, Lululemon has also become synonymous with women's performance apparel. Therefore, I will call a 'Strong Buy' rating for Lululemon.

Risk to Investment Thesis

Despite my belief that Lululemon possesses a moat, this doesn't entirely suggest that the company is immune to competition. Lululemon faces significant competition from multiple fronts in the apparel industry. Competition comes from traditional performance apparel brands like Nike, Adidas, and Under Armor (UA), which compete with Lululemon in terms of product features, brand, and price. There's also a threat from fast fashion, whose core value proposition is their ability to rapidly introduce new designs at a significantly lower price. Even Costco (COST), of all companies, was sued by Lululemon for allegedly selling a knockoff product. This wide-ranging source of competition indicates low barriers to entry and makes Lululemon's dedication to innovation and newness even more imperative. After all, they must justify the premium they are charging with an equivalent feature or specification. There's also a significant risk in the company's international expansion. Despite my belief in the company's prospects in the international market, the disparity between these countries' fashion tastes due to cultural differences and ethnicity may make them immune to the formula that made Lululemon such a success in North America.

data.title: Klook Technology Targets U.S. IPO On Strong Growth
data.description:
Klook Is Growing Quickly And Approaching Operating Breakeven

Klook Technology Limited (KLK) has filed to raise $100 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, according to SEC F-1 registration information.

KLK operates a travel booking platform in Asia for a wide variety of experiences.

The firm is growing revenue quickly in the wake of the pandemic travel drop and is approaching operating breakeven, producing impressive results in growing markets.

I’ll provide an update when we learn more IPO details from management.

What Does Klook Do?

Singapore-based Klook Technology Limited was founded to develop an online platform for the 'global experiences economy' focused on the Asia Pacific market.

Management is led by co-founder, Chairman and CEO Ethan Lin, who has been with the firm since its inception and was previously in the investment banking group of Standard Chartered and at Citi.

The company's primary offerings include its mobile-first platform, especially focused on last-mile experiences booking and instant confirmation to connect a wide range of operators with consumers.

Revenue contribution by segment and region is shown in the pie charts below:

Klook has booked fair market value investment of $66.3 million as of September 30, 2025, from investors, including EEB Capital Limited, HSG affiliates, MPC entities, Softbank entity and Knight Taano entity.

What Is Klook’s Market?

According to a 2022 market research report by Grand View Research, the Asia Pacific market for travel booking was an estimated $140 billion in 2021 and was forecast to exceed $324 billion by 2030.

This represents a projected CAGR of 9.8% from 2022 to 2030, a relatively strong figure.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are transportation bookings, which represented the largest revenue source in 2021. Accommodation booking is expected to produce the highest growth rate of all segments through 2030.

Also, the chart below shows the historical and projected future growth path of the Asia Pacific online travel booking service market through 2030:

Major competitive vendors or industry players include the following firms:

  • Agoda

  • Traveloka

The competitive landscape features smaller players focused on individual countries, mid-size participants with a wider focus and larger, more horizontal players like Klook with a broad multi-country network.

The company operates a mobile-first online marketplace that attracts users via its digital marketing efforts and word of mouth.

As of the twelve months ended September 30, 2025, the company said it booked over 65 million "experiences" and had over 310,000 experience offerings at over 4,200 destinations.

Below is a thumbnail SWOT analysis for Klook:

Strengths

  • Largest pan-APAC platform, high organic marketing reach, mobile-first platform.

Weaknesses

  • Operating complexity with SME operators, dependence on mobile and social discovery, seasonal revenue and working capital needs.

Opportunities

  • Growth potential in India and Japan, generative AI-enhanced services, strategic partnerships, adjacent travel services and bundling.

Threats

  • VIE legal structures in PRC and Taiwan are contractual only and subject to unpredictable regulatory changes, listing and compliance uncertainties.

The firm’s performance as a percentage of revenue is shown in the chart below:

The chart indicates that gross margin has improved significantly over time, while Sales and Marketing expenses have varied within a range and R&D expenses have declined somewhat.

Klook’s IPO Plan

KLK intends to sell Class A ordinary shares in an IPO that could reach $400 million in size, not including the sale of customary underwriter options.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares at the IPO price, at least as divulged in the initial filing.

Class A stockholders will receive one vote per share.

The co-founders will receive Class B shares that entitle them to twenty (20 votes) per share and the company will be a ‘controlled company’ immediately post-IPO.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the typical 180-day period following the effective date of the IPO prospectus.

According to Klook’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

The principal purposes of this offering are to increase our capitalization and financial flexibility and to create a public market for our ADSs. We currently intend to use the net proceeds we receive from this offering for general corporate purposes, including working capital, operating expenses and capital expenditures. We cannot specify with certainty all of the particular uses for the remaining net proceeds from this offering. We may also use a portion of the net proceeds for acquisitions of, or strategic investments in, complementary businesses, products, services or technologies. However, we do not have any current plans or commitments to enter into any material acquisitions or investments at this time.

(Source - SEC.)

The firm’s presentation of the company roadshow is not yet available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, management said the firm is not currently a party to any material legal proceedings.

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Goldman Sachs, J.P. Morgan, Morgan Stanley, Citi, Mizuho, RBC Capital Markets, Oppenheimer & Co. and Wedbush Securities.

Klook Is Growing Quickly And Nearing Operating Breakeven

Klook is seeking U.S. public capital market investment to fund its ambitious growth plans.

The firm’s financials have produced increasing topline revenue, growing gross profit and gross margin, lower operating losses but fluctuating cash flow from or use in operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended September 30, 2025, was $8.2 million.

Selling and Marketing expenses as a percentage of total revenue have trended unevenly higher as revenue has grown even more quickly and its Selling and Marketing efficiency multiple almost doubled to 0.7x in the most recent reporting period, a positive trend.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends in the foreseeable future and will retain any earnings and available cash for its growth initiatives and working capital requirements.

The higher recent net loss has been a function of the rising non-cash value of the convertible promissory notes, which existing investors may redeem for more cash or convert to a greater number of shares in the future. IFRS requires the company to book the loss (or gain) for the valuation changes now.

KLK’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent moderately on capital expenditures as a percentage of its operating cash flow.

Share-based compensation as a percentage of free cash flow was 131% over the trailing twelve-month period.

The firm’s growth strategy includes expanding its efforts in India and Japan, adding adjacent service offerings, improving user experience through generative AI capabilities and pursuing strategic partnerships.

The market opportunity for providing travel experiences in the Asia Pacific region is expected to grow at nearly 10% CAGR through 2030, so the company has positive growth dynamics in its favor.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its "foreign private issuer" and "emerging growth company" status, each of which enables management to reduce its information-sharing requirements with investors.

Many of these types of company stocks, especially Asia-based companies, have performed poorly post-IPO.

The company has regional concentration risks within the APAC region, working capital intensity during seasonal peak travel periods and is dependent on social media-led traffic subject to external platform changes.

KLK is growing impressively while making meaningful progress toward operating breakeven, so the IPO may be in substantial demand from institutional investors.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Novo: The Double Bottom Is In
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Novo Nordisk (NVO) delivered a mixed Q3, and the stock continues to hover near all-time lows. But beneath the noise, the long-term story continues to be compelling, with strength in obesity drugs and a good pipeline of upcoming products.

Novo remains one of the most compelling long-term compounders in global healthcare, though there are still significant risks associated with this stock and segment.

Technically, this stock may have put in a double bottom, and with the worst now behind us in terms of earnings, this is the time to buy aggressively. I previously covered NVO in August, and I still maintain my "Strong Buy" rating.

Novo Q3

Q3 revenue grew 11% to DKK 75.0 billion.

This was driven by strong sales in GLP-1, up +11% YoY and Obesity care +18%.

But the headline number everyone saw was the 30% drop in operating profit, which looks alarming until you realize it was almost entirely caused by DKK 9 billion in restructuring charges, including 9,000 layoffs and R&D impairments.

Without taking these into account, operating profit would have actually increased by +7% in local currency.

Similarly, this would have changed the outlook on margins.

But Wegovy is still crushing it globally up +54% CER, and demand outside the US is exploding (APAC +221% CER).

In terms of guidance, Novo did bring things down, with 2025 sales expected to grow 8%–11% and operating profit: 4%–7%. Novo has also recently accepted the Medicare MFP price cap for semaglutide starting in 2027, which will impact future margins.

Why I Like Novo

Despite the guidance cut and headline noise, three pillars of the Novo thesis remain exceptionally strong.

Global obesity demand is still going parabolic

The global branded obesity market grew 136% over the last 12 months, and Novo holds 59% of the global volume share despite competition from Eli Lilly.

International Wegovy uptake is the biggest new driver:

  • EUCAN +65%

  • APAC +221%

  • China launched and is ramping up. This is the largest therapeutic market expansion in modern history.

The pipeline is being rebuilt for the next decade

Novo is setting itself up not just for semaglutide, but a multi-mechanism obesity and cardiometabolic portfolio:

  • Cagrilintide Phase 3 initiated

  • Oral semaglutide 25mg submitted in US/EU

  • Oral amycretin and subcutaneous amycretin going to Phase 3

  • Semaglutide for MASH submitted in EU/Japan

  • Acquiring Akero (FGF21) + Omeros (MASP-3)

Novo is not just a GLP-1 company. It’s one of the best and largest pharma companies out there with a proven track record.

The Wegovy pill is the next growth S-curve

New reports confirm Weight Watchers will sell Novo's oral Wegovy pill, perhaps the most important GLP-1 catalyst since 2021.

If semaglutide becomes oral and mass-market, it will have widespread implications. Coverage will expand, and adherence will improve.

Asking someone to inject themselves with Wegovy is one thing, but to just take a pill? This could unlock another wave of global obesity penetration, especially in low-friction markets (US cash pay, Asia, Latin America).

Manufacturing scale is a real moat

Some investors complain that the CAPEX is too high (DKK ~60 billion expected in 2025), but it creates a unique defensive advantage.

More unique APIs, control over supply, and faster global launches and distribution. No company, except maybe Eli Lilly (LLY) is scaling at this level.

Valuation

Valuation remains compelling, even by the harshest metrics.

Yes, forward earnings growth will be bad, at least for a few quarters, but even taking this into account, NVO trades at a fair price.

Forward Price/Cash Flow is 16, which is reasonable and in line with the sector median.

For a market leader, this is actually a discount.

The downside is quite limited, while the upside, as Novo executes a turnaround, could be significant.

Technical Analysis

On a technical level, I’d argue that we have a pretty compelling double bottom in place, with the weekly RSI also giving us a bullish divergence and the MACD ready to flip positive again.

Clearly, the market will go ahead of itself in 2024, but we’re now at price levels of 2022, and again, I don’t see much downside left.

Risks

Novo Nordisk faces several risks that could affect its growth over the next few years.

The most immediate is pricing and reimbursement pressure in the United States, where Wegovy and Ozempic are already seeing lower realized prices and tightening coverage, particularly within Medicaid.

The upcoming Medicare Maximum Fair Price (MFP) rule in 2027 adds another layer of uncertainty, as negotiated caps on semaglutide could create a structural drag on revenue.

Competition is intensifying too, with Eli Lilly’s tirzepatide taking share in both diabetes and obesity.

And let’s not forget that the semaglutide patent expires from 2026 onwards, which represents a longer-term headwind, especially outside the US.

Final Thoughts

While Novo faces challenges, I think the market has priced in the worst already and is giving us a chance to buy a great company at a fair price.

Novo will be in business for decades, and its products will continue to generate demand globally.

At today’s price, Novo is fairly valued and pays a 3% yield. This is a no-brainer addition to a diversified portfolio, in my opinion.

data.title: Evolution Petroleum: A Breakeven Quarter And $50 Million Of Debt (Downgrade)
data.description:
Evolution Petroleum Corporation (EPM) reported $53 million of long-term debt while earning $.02 per share. This was a far cry from the usual debt-free balance sheet reported in the past. There were some assimilation costs in the latest quarter from an acquisition, combined with some nonrecurring issues, to push earnings down. The main concern here is that this company does not have low operating costs as a general rule. During periods of weak (oil) pricing (like now), that can put cash flow at risk, just as the company went on a shopping spree. Offsetting some of the oil price (CL1:COM) weakness was strengthening natural gas prices (NG1:COM) that are expected to recover some more. Nonetheless, the risk of this idea is higher now than it's ever been due to that debt load and weak oil prices.

Map Of Operations

With three acquisitions since the beginning of fiscal year 2024, this company has materially expanded its operations.

This also marks the first time that the company has ventured into the minerals interest (or royalty interest) business. In the past, the company has acquired existing production during periods of weak pricing and then waited for the ensuing cyclical recovery to financially deleverage. That appears to be the case now. However, long-term debt has never been this high before.

Debt Ratio

As the following slide demonstrates, the debt ratio remains conservative at the end of the fiscal year. However, there were some issues this quarter that resulted in the weak earnings report. That may mean, to a certain extent, the beginning of the deleveraging depends upon a few decent quarters to establish more breathing room.

With the completion of the latest acquisition in August, net debt is now at $53 million. That is a good deal higher than the $37.5 million level announced at the end of the fiscal year.

In the meantime, adjusted EBITDA has not grown with the latest acquisitions.

Hopefully, the next quarter will reflect the accretive nature of the last two acquisitions that occurred in April and August. But as shown above, weaker commodity prices and likely some nonrecurring charges that go with the acquisitions are dominating the adjusted EBITDA trend.

It would appear that the fiscal year 2026 first quarter adjusted EBITDA annualized would put the debt ratio closer to 2 than 1. That may not be terrible compared to years past in this industry. But it is still quite a stretch for a high-cost operator like this one.

Dividend History

This company has cut the dividend before when cash flow was low. Therefore, shareholders can expect something like that happening again if commodity prices weaken to a level where such an action is indicated.

In the latest quarter, management declared the dividend, as it has in the past few quarters. But with the history shown above, this is best looked at as a variable distribution entity.

Therefore, while the history is pretty good, it may be best to consider this either during a recession or after the threat of recession has passed.

While the recent acquisitions bode well for a higher future dividend, if a position is established now, it may be wise to consider keeping some of the investment money available for a better deal down the road.

Latest GDP Forecast

The Federal Reserve Bank Of Atlanta shows the GDP recovering from an unexpected hiccup to return to historical levels of economic growth. Note that the blue chip group surveyed is far less optimistic about this.

Commodity prices with the WTI below $60 currently reflect the weak outlook for the economy. Therefore, commodity prices are not as optimistic as the Federal Reserve Bank is about the economic outlook.

This may be important as it relates to future cash flows that enable dividend payments by Evolution Petroleum, as the company is a fairly high-cost operator. It is therefore a bit more sensitive to weaker oil prices than is the case for some lower cost operators.

Evolution can be as disciplined as it wants to be about buying established production. But the risk here comes from financial leverage, not the disciplined purchases. It still needs a minimum amount of cash flow to deleverage. In the immediate future, the initial deleveraging could be slower than anticipated if the economic outlook weakens to a recession (or worse).

Summary

The debt level would seem to indicate a much higher risk than was the case for this high-cost operator. Before now, the low or no debt policy offered considerable financial safety during any downturn. Right now, that would not appear to be the case.

However, this company is more diversified than it has ever been in the past. That may make up for some of the financial risk that the current amount of long-term debt would imply.

Still, this has become a speculative idea that I would currently downgrade to a hold because of the economic uncertainty combined with the current debt levels. Buying during a time when there are few buyers does have its advantages. But this time around, the economic uncertainty makes the proposition of a future deleveraging a bit dicey.

Conservative investors may want to sell this and consider something with less debt. This is now for more risk-on investors.

Risks

If the economic uncertainty dissipates without a period of weak (or weaker) oil prices followed by another period of high oil prices, then this company could deleverage relatively fast without any "bumps or bruises."

On the other side of the Hold idea is the fact that this company is a higher cost operator. It is far more sensitive to the drop of really any commodity prices. In this case, the immediate threat is weak oil prices. But if there is a delay in the recovery of natural gas prices or even a weakening of natural gas prices combined with the current oil price outlook, this company could be in for some tight quarters until the cyclical recovery begins.

In addition to the commodity price outlook, there were some operational issues that were reported in the latest quarters that could also affect cash flow if they happen at the wrong time.

data.title: First Horizon Undervalued, But Operational Positives Clouded By M&A And Macro Concerns
data.description:
This year has been up and down for First Horizon Corporation (FHN). While this large regional bank has done well from an operational perspective, with better-than-expected earnings and solid credit, investors have been spooked by management’s apparent willingness to consider M&A (as a buyer) and ongoing worries about loan demand amid sluggish business capex spending.

First Horizon shares are up close to 10% since my last update, not particularly exciting relative to the S&P 500, but better than the average regional bank (down slightly) and better than many of its Southeastern peers/comps.

I still like First Horizon’s strategic positioning, and I believe the shares can continue to recover from the sell-off prompted by management’s comments on M&A (which they’ve since tried to clarify and contextualize). While the stock isn’t tremendously undervalued with fair value in the mid-$20’s, I still think it’s a better-than-average pick in the space.

M&A Worries: To Buy, Or Be Bought?

First Horizon shares sold off sharply despite better-than-expected third quarter earnings, due to management’s comments that they believed they had the capacity and potential willingness to be a buyer in the bank space on a limited basis.

This wasn’t actually a new comment (management had said something similar about a month previously), but with First Horizon frequently at or near the top of sell-side analyst “most likely banks to be acquired” lists, the Street got spooked. Moreover, with Fifth Third (FITB) announcing the acquisition of Comerica (CMA) (and Huntington’s (HBAN) later acquisition of Cadence (CADE), two of the more likely potential FHN acquirers dropped off the board.

To a point, this worry is understandable, as bank acquirers have tended to underperform for around 12–24 months after announcing deals, with many banks struggling to deliver the promised accretion that justified the deals.

Still, I think it’s much ado about not all that much. For starters, and as bank management has since clarified in subsequent presentations to analysts/investors, a capacity and willingness to do a deal is not the same thing as actively hunting for a bank to buy. Moreover, First Horizon likely isn’t looking for a large transformative deal – rather, a tuck-in transaction that would boost the bank’s presence in a market (or markets) like Charlotte, Dallas, Houston, Raleigh, and/or Tampa. I think this is particularly true given that recent announced acquisitions like the Pinnacle (PNFP) – Synovus (SNV) deal could create some opportunities for competitive depositor and loan officer takeaways.

Given respectable share in attractive markets like Florida, North Carolina, and Tennessee, as well as solid underlying fundamentals and above-average leverage to commercial lending, I still believe that First Horizon could be an attractive target for a bank that wants to accelerate its expansion into the Southeast. A deal of its own could delay that process, but I still think First Horizon nets out as an attractive and likely acquisition target in a sector where M&A activity has definitely picked up.

Core Performance Compares Well

Through the bank’s third quarter results (where the bank reported beats at the revenue, pre-provision operating profit, and adjusted EPS lines), First Horizon continues to compare well to many of its peers.

Although loan growth has been a little weaker than the average of late (average loans were up 0.6% YoY and 0.4% QoQ in Q3 vs. 0.7% QoQ average growth in the peer group), the net interest margin of 3.55% (or 3.48% adjusted) is nearly a quarter-point above average, with better deposit costs and slightly better asset yields. Credit is also holding up relatively well – at around 1.4% First Horizon is better-reserved than many of its peers (averaging around 1.2%), but its non-performing and charge-off ratios are more or less in line.

First Horizon is a little more asset-sensitive than average, which isn’t as helpful at this point in the rate cycle, but the bank still has attractive fixed income trading exposure. Management has noted that regional deposit competition has eased up, but commercial lending is still soft as companies hold off on capex/investment commitments given ongoing uncertainty about tariffs and government policies.

Looking at credit, I’m not concerned about the bank’s commercial real estate exposure today. CRE lending is about 22% of total lending, but the book is diverse across industries and regions, and most of its office exposure (about 19% of CRE loans) is in medical offices. While the CRE non-performing asset ratio has been ticking up (from 1.76% a year ago and 1.70% in Q2’25 to 1.86% in Q3), the charge-off ratio is fine so far (0.09%).

The one area that does concern me some is its exposure to non-depository financial companies – there has been increasing credit pressure on non-bank lenders to consumers (across mortgage, auto, and personal lending categories), and about 12% of FHN’s loans go to this category. Mitigating this to some extent is the fact that this exposure is largely on the mortgage side, where there’s better collateral coverage.

The Outlook

Over the last year, First Horizon has not only logged multiple quarterly beats, but also raised its return on tangible common equity expectations from a target of 15% to 15% now being considered the acceptable minimum. There’s still work to do on the deposit side (the loan/deposit ratio is high at 95%), but deposit quality is good, and the bank’s market share has been improving in multiple major markets (Texas has proven a challenging market, while North Carolina and Florida have been doing better).

I’m still looking for around 6% core earnings growth over the next five years and 5% over the longer term, and management has been good about returning capital to shareholders (the share count has dropped about 5% in the last year). Putting capital toward M&A is a risk, particularly for sentiment, but again, I’d expect any deal to be relatively modest in size and targeted on specific growth markets (like Dallas/Houston or Raleigh).

Discounting the core earnings back, I think First Horizon should trade closer to $24/share right now. I get a similar result with my ROTCE-driven T/PBV approach (a 14% ROTCE in FY’26 would support a 1.65x TBV multiple) and a 12.5x multiple to my ’26 EPS multiple (more in line with current multiples for similar banks).

The Bottom Line

Given ongoing sluggishness in core loan demand and a wobbly economy, I’m by no means wildly bullish about First Horizon’s pre-provision profit growth potential over the next year, even though a steeper yield curve should be good for the sector in general. While FHN’s growth in FY’26 could be a bit below the group average, the quality is still there (a mid-teens ROTCE), and the valuation is not demanding. As a solid GARP idea on its own, and with some M&A upside potential, I think this is a name that at least deserves some consideration for investors looking to add regional bank exposure.

data.title: Upwork: Attractive Entry Price That Could Become Even Better In A Recession
data.description:
While I have been aware of Upwork (UPWK) for years, I decided to give it a try as a user just a few days ago by creating an account to see if it would be worth doing some freelance work on the platform.

After creating an account and setting up the profile, I found it really user-friendly, easy to navigate, and a great concept overall. However, two things struck me straight away.

One is that for most job offers, there are over 20 applicants, and the other is the aggressive monetization strategy (more on that later). After doubting a bit about its worthiness as a freelancer due to all the fees and the need to pay even to apply for a job, I decided to look for reviews, and to my surprise, they were quite positive.

The combination of a positive user experience despite the aggressive monetization strategy led me to think that Upwork probably has something interesting from a business perspective going on.

The go-to freelancing marketplace

Upwork is the largest marketplace connecting freelancers from a wide variety of industries with companies looking to have a particular job done.

It has a clear and easy-to-understand value proposition, which is something I value when researching a company. It provides a pool of freelancers with a proven track record (reviews, jobs done, verification, and hourly rates) to companies and makes life easier for freelancers looking for a job. All offers are on one site, payments are guaranteed, and Upwork mediates in case of discrepancies.

In my view, the biggest competitive advantage is its network effect. Companies will look for talent on the site with the larger user base, and freelancers benefit from Upwork's extensive job offerings and search engines.

This strong competitive advantage is probably the result of outstanding execution plus a first-mover advantage. Most of Upwork's competitors don't come even close, and some are more focused on a particular market, such as design. The only competitor that could be viewed as a threat is Fiverr, but despite having similar website traffic and charging higher fees than Upwork, it generates half of the revenues.

Road to profitability, a classic tech story

Over most of its history, Upwork has been unprofitable, staying alive through debt issuances, dilution, and paying a substantial amount of salaries with stocks.

However, SG&A expenses peaked at $370M in 2024, and since then, revenues have increased by 26.3%, while SG&A expenses have declined by 25% to $277M over the last twelve months.

As a result, Upwork has become free cash flow positive even excluding stock-based compensation, which is also declining, and I view it as great news. As shown in the chart below, the only expense line that was not decreasing in recent years is R&D, although it started to do so during 2025 ($138M vs $155M during the first 9 months).

The strategy of aggressively spending on customer acquisition and gaining market share to reduce costs thereafter was probably already on Upwork's roadmap, but I believe the company is heavily benefiting from AI. It doesn't need to spend crazy amounts of capital on infrastructure, but when looking at the employee count, the company decreased its headcount from 800 internal employees by the end of 2023 to 600 by the end of 2024.

Over 35% of our deployed code is now AI generated. And in our Search team, fine-tuned LLM evaluations of match quality have reduced our model iteration time and cost by over 70%.

Source: Hayden Brown (CEO).

Although revenue growth has significantly slowed from double-digit rates in recent years to 4% in Q3 2025, Upwork has achieved record adjusted EBITDA margins of 30%, which, in my view, clearly proves improved efficiency.

Those readers who have followed my research for some time will know I am not a big fan of "adjusted" figures. However, I don't see a problem of using Upwork's adjusted EBITDA as an indicator at this point, since they are adding back SBC (which is declining anyway, so it is a headwind for adjusted EBITDA) and taxes, which I believe is important to do so at the current stage, given the substantial tax variance due to tax benefits from previous years' losses.

Adjusted EBITDA margins have expanded from close to zero until 2022, to 10% in 2023, and 22% in 2024. For now, the margin expansion trend doesn't seem to slow down, so the main question is, what margins can Upwork achieve long term?

The management has stated that they are targeting a long-term adjusted EBITDA margin of 35%, a figure they raised from the previous 30% target, and they expect to achieve it before 2028.

Squeezing the platform and labor demand

To achieve this significant margin expansion, the company is not just cutting costs, but is also squeezing the platform as much as possible.

As a user, this is something one quickly realizes when joining the platform. Upwork uses a credit system where you need to buy credits (Connects) to apply for jobs.

The cost of credits is around $15 for 100 credits, and while the number of credits required to apply for a job varies depending on the demand, project length, and expected earnings, it is around 15 credits per application.

On top of that, Upwork takes a 10% rate from the freelancer's earnings, charges companies for publishing offers, user verification, and increasing visibility.

In my view, one of the most interesting aspects of Upwork's business model is that it serves as a natural hedge against inflation, since as labor prices go up, it doesn't need to adjust its rates, as it charges a percentage of the gross value.

On the negative side, it is highly exposed to labor demand. Since 70% of the companies that hire through Upwork are from the US, I don't consider it a highly defensive business, and I expect significant volatility in Upwork's earnings along with economic cycles.

Upwork's valuation

At the moment, Upwork has a market cap of $2.34B, $0.64B in cash, and $0.36M in debt maturing in August 2026. The debt will likely be paid in cash and won't dilute further the share count, since it was structured as convertible notes with a conversion price of $66, but holders can choose to receive cash. Excluding net cash, Upwork is valued at around $2B.

Year to date, including SBC expenses, Upwork has generated close to $100M of net income, which adds to $130M when annualized. Note that 2024's net income figures are distorted by a one-time tax benefit and merger expenses, so they should not be used as a reference.

Looking ahead, I use a conservative revenue growth assumption of mid single digits, in line with real GDP growth plus inflation, and adjusted EBITDA margins reaching management's target, albeit I believe the company has room to expand them further if it can maintain its competitive position by increasing its TAM, monetization through ads, and take rate expansion (currently at 19.8%, which I believe is impressive).

While I acknowledge that there might be significant volatility in relation to revenues if the US economy enters a recession, I believe a 4-5% revenue growth rate on average over the long term is fair.

I am using a 12% discount rate to add a substantial margin of safety, as I don't view Upwork as a defensive business and it has a limited track record.

However, despite these conservative assumptions, the stock is trading at a 22% discount to its fair value, and using a 15x LTM exit multiple (assuming no multiple expansion), Upwork's stock would deliver a 12% CAGR over the next decade.

As shown in the model presented above, I do not add back SBC to free cash flow. Therefore, I do not account for share dilution from stock-based compensation.

The management is deploying its excess cash into buybacks, which I view favorably given the low CapEx requirements due to the nature of its business and the current valuation. This year so far, it has spent $100M in buybacks, which represents a 4-5% decrease in the share count. Given that I do not assume a valuation multiple expansion, I forecast a 4% decline in share count going forward to estimate 2035's FCF per share.

Risks to consider

While I view Upwork as a reasonable investment at the current price, it is hard to assess the impact that a severe recession would have on its results. From a revenue perspective, it is clear to me that they would decline by double digits, given the short-term nature of the contracts between companies and freelancers.

Although the company is looking to boost relations with larger corporations and longer-term jobs, the reality is that many jobs published on the platform are completed in a few days or weeks, so companies would reduce their budget on Upwork much faster than with their internal workforce.

For now, despite the decline in job offerings, the company has been able to grow revenues by squeezing as much as possible from its platform, but if unemployment starts to increase, Upwork's valuation could decline further.

On the other hand, I do not share the fear that AI will reduce the demand for the platform, since it only affects the simplest jobs for now, which have lower hourly rates, and could boost demand for AI/related jobs.

So the bigger impact for us from AI is actually that AI is causing the fractionalization of traditional full-time work, meaning new opportunities are emerging where businesses no longer need a full-time person because AI is doing part of the work, but they do need someone to help augment that work.

Source: Hayden Brown (CEO)

In my view, the biggest threat would be that large tech companies such as Meta (META) or Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) try to compete in this space, since they have a large customer base and capital to quickly take a significant market share. For now, however, they seem focused on the AI race.

Takeaway

Although Upwork's revenue growth is slowing down and the company might face significant headwinds in an economic slowdown, the company is attractively priced and holds a dominant position in a market with network effects.

From my point of view, Upwork is trading below its fair value even using conservative assumptions and a 12% discount rate, and it should deliver double-digit returns over the long term.

However, the company might present an even better opportunity for long-term investors if the economy enters a recession, since Upwork's margin expansion path would be halted, leading to a decline in valuation and an even better entry price.

data.title: Market Cycles, Not Headlines: What History Says About The 2025 Rally And What Comes Next
data.description:
This article was originally published on I/O Fund on November 06, 2025

By Knox Ridley, Portfolio Manager

Despite how it may seem, modern-day narratives rarely drive market swings. Tariffs, political headlines, niche trends like rare earth materials, or speculation about which company OpenAI partners with next — these stories dominate the news cycle, but they do not reliably move markets, as the consensus believes. If they did, investing would be much easier.

Numerous well-known studies have come to this conclusion. One of the most famous is “What Moves Stock Prices?” by Harvard and MIT economists Cutler, Poterba, and Summers. Their goal was to model how news and macroeconomic events might predict stock market movements. To their surprise, they found that only about one-third of major price swings could be linked to identifiable news events.

This finding was later reinforced by Yale economist Ray Fair in his groundbreaking paper, “Events That Shook the Market.” Fair examined major one-day movements in the S&P 500 from 1950 to 1999 in search of their causes. He concluded that neither news, earnings, nor data releases could explain most of these large jumps. As Fair put it, “It is difficult to find any news that corresponds to many of the largest daily changes in stock prices.”

We’ve seen this phenomenon play out in real time. The COVID crash was perhaps the most striking example. Economic data went off the charts: 26 million Americans filed for unemployment within five weeks, as GDP fell 31.4% annualized — the steepest drop since World War II. Yet the stock market bottomed at the height of this deterioration and uncertainty, staging a V-shaped recovery that was impossible to justify by the data alone.

There are many forces that shape markets — liquidity, growth, and monetary and fiscal policy among them. But there are also powerful, less tangible forces that economics struggle to explain. One of these is herd sentiment, which we explored in last week’s report, Decoding the S&P 500: When Human Sentiment Meets Artificial Intelligence.

This week, we’ll turn to another underappreciated but potent influence on markets - cycles. Much like natural phenomena, financial markets move in rhythmic, repetitive patterns that can be observed, analyzed, and applied to better understand broader trends.

Understanding market cycles is essential for anyone seeking to interpret market behavior beyond the noise of daily headlines. While news and data provide short-term context, the deeper rhythm of expansion, contraction, and renewal has repeated throughout centuries of market history. These cycles reflect the underlying forces of liquidity, sentiment, credit, and innovation that collectively drive long-term trends. By studying them, investors can gain perspective — identifying where we may be in the broader sequence of optimism and fear — and making decisions grounded in historical precedent rather than emotion.

In this report, we will look at two dominant cycles that closely align with the price action in 2025. Both suggest a potential year-end rally, followed by the potential for volatility into Q1 of 2026. We’ll then line these cycles up with the broad market to outline what levels must hold, and what targets this uptrend is hitting, as we push higher.

The Gann Cycle Framework: Predictable Rhythms Behind Market Movements

The concept of market cycles gained mainstream attention through Neil Howe and William Strauss’s theory The Fourth Turning, which proposes that history unfolds in recurring 80–100-year cycles based on generational shifts. Each “turning” reflects a distinct societal mood—ranging from confidence and expansion to crisis and renewal—that repeatedly shapes political, economic, and market behavior.

However, the study of cycles long predates Howe and Strauss. In 1862, economist Karl Juglar identified what became known as the Juglar Cycle—an 8 to 11-year rhythm of expansion and contraction that still appears in modern market data. Juglar’s work established the foundation for viewing markets not as random systems, but as recurring patterns driven by predictable phases of human and economic behavior.

Decades later, W.D. Gann advanced this concept into one of the most comprehensive frameworks for understanding market structure. Gann demonstrated through decades of analysis that market movements often unfold in rhythmic, repeating patterns tied to both human psychology and natural law. His research suggested that the same behavioral and structural forces that shaped past bull and bear markets continue to influence markets today.

Gann identified several “master cycles”—notably the 100-year, 90-year, 60-year, 52-year, 45-year, and 30-year cycles. At any given time, one or more of these cycles, as well as divisions of these cycles, tend to influence the prevailing market trend. He used these relationships to issue remarkably accurate forecasts, many of which have held up over time.

While this may sound abstract, it holds historical merit. For example, 90 years back from the 1929 market top takes us to the end of the 1839 speculative boom, which ended in the Panic of 1839. Counting 90 years forward from 1929 brings us within months of the COVID top in 2020—an equally significant inflection point.

Another example is Gann’s 60-year “Great Cycle.” When we overlay the S&P 500 from 1962 onto today’s market starting in January 2022, the patterns align with remarkable similarity. While cycles can invert or distort temporarily, they consistently identify key inflection points and general directional bias.

Because sentiment moves in waves, the emotional extremes of fear and greed remain timeless. While technology, policy, and liquidity conditions evolve, the human response to opportunity and risk does not. Even if equities rise in 2025 on optimism surrounding AI, investor psychology mirrors that of prior generations—driven by the same patterns of exuberance and denial.

To see how these long-term cycles manifest in real markets, let’s look at two historical periods that mirror 2025 with uncanny precision.

With this in mind, we examined historical precedents for 2025—a year defined by a rapid 20% decline in Q1 that erased nearly all of 2024’s gains, followed by a strong seven-month rally with limited pullbacks. These conditions—a liquidity shock followed by a sharp rebound—are rare. Over two centuries of data, only two market periods fit this mold: 1980 and 1998. Interestingly, both align with significant Gann cycles—the 45-year (half of the 90-year) and the 26-year (half of the 52-year) cycles.

To summarize: History may not repeat — but it often rhymes.

The 45-Year Market Cycle: How 1980’s Policy Shock Is Repeating in 2025

The market in 1980 has a striking resemblance to what we’ve seen unfold in 2025. In both periods, a swift and unexpected policy shock triggered a sudden liquidity event that sent markets sharply lower, followed by a rapid V-shaped recovery that defied expectations.

In early 1980, the Federal Reserve—newly under the leadership of Paul Volcker—launched an aggressive campaign to control inflation. The Fed pushed interest rates to nearly 17% in February, an unprecedented move that instantly drained liquidity from the system and sent equity markets into a sharp correction.

Similarly, in early 2025, the executive branch imposed an unexpected increase in tariffs, creating a sudden liquidity squeeze that rippled across U.S. markets. The February decline that followed erased much of the prior year’s gains in a matter of weeks.

In both years, the shock proved temporary. Political pressure and a seizing credit market forced Volcker to reverse course in 1980, cutting rates back to roughly 9 percent. The result was an eight-month recovery that lasted through late November. Likewise, in 2025, just days after Liberation Day, the bond market began a disorderly unwind that pushed yields higher than the government could sustain. The policy reversal that followed restored liquidity and fueled a seven-month rally that continues today.

If this historical rhythm continues to guide market behavior, the current advance could extend through December, leading to a secondary high in mid-January followed by a period of elevated volatility into the second quarter of 2026.

The 26-Year Market Cycle: How 1998’s Global Crisis Echoes Through 2025’s Rally

When we align the 26-year cycle with the 2025 decline, the parallels are difficult to ignore. The 1998 correction was sparked by a sudden global liquidity crisis that rippled through international markets. It began with the Asian Financial Crisis, as currencies in Thailand, Indonesia, and South Korea collapsed under the pressure of capital flight. The shock then spread to Russia, which defaulted on its domestic debt, setting off a global contagion.

One of the largest casualties was Long-Term Capital Management, a highly leveraged hedge fund managed by Nobel laureates and veteran Wall Street traders. As LTCM’s positions unraveled, liquidity evaporated across credit markets. Within two months, U.S. equities had dropped roughly 20 percent, forcing the Federal Reserve to intervene and backstop the system.

The Fed’s swift action reignited risk appetite, setting off one of the most powerful rallies in modern market history. Fueled by speculation about the transformative potential of the internet, investors poured into technology and growth stocks, propelling the market into the final and most euphoric phase of the dot-com boom.

While today’s AI leaders—such as Nvidia—are built on far stronger fundamentals than the speculative favorites of 2000, like Cisco and Pets.com, the behavioral pattern is strikingly similar. Both eras were defined by optimism surrounding an emerging technology with vast, untested potential and by investors’ willingness to price in future revolutions before they materialized.

If this 26-year cycle continues to guide the market, the current uptrend could extend through December and into the first quarter of 2026 before encountering the first meaningful correction. Unlike the 45-year cycle, however, this correction would likely be brief—more of a consolidation within a broader advance that carries into late 2026.

Interestingly, both cycles suggest continued strength into December, and both cycles suggest some period of volatility in Q1 of 2026. What separates the two is how far this uptrend pushes into Q1. While the 45-year cycle suggests a top in mid-December and lower high into mid-January, the 26-year cycle suggests a continuation of the aggressive uptrend into mid-Q1 of next year before seeing a small period of volatility.

Broad Market Levels

If we take the general direction of the above cycles and place it within the context of developing price patterns, there are two counts that best fit:

One of these scenarios is starting to take shape — read this timely analysis below.

  • Blue – We are in the final swings of the rally off the April 2025 low. As long as any weakness holds SPX 6,552.50 – 6345, then we should see this rally continue into year-end, before seeing any notable volatility unfold into Q1 of 2026.
  • Green – This path follows the blue path above. The difference is that the Q1 period of volatility will likely only be a correction within a larger uptrend, which would then continue into 2026.

In conclusion, as with all cyclical analysis, no one can say with certainty why a particular cycle takes hold or how long it will remain dominant. Markets often follow one rhythm until it loses influence, at which point a new cycle emerges and becomes the primary driver. The task for us as analysts is to identify which cycle is in control and the conditions that will sustain it.

At this stage, the 45-year and 26-year cycles appear to be the prevailing forces. As long as the SPX holds between 6,552.50 and 6,345 on any near-term weakness, the market is likely to continue tracking these patterns, both of which point toward the potential for continued strength into year-end.
